sentences.text: Yeah.
And there was in [INAUDIBLE] that in the middle of January, the middle of winter, which was a big snow, very cold nights, maybe minus 15, minus 20.
When the Russians got very close and then one morning, around the 18th of January '45, at 4 o'clock in the morning, the SS men burst into every building, into every room.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Pigs, get out, Jewish pigs.
Everybody out on the main square.
Anybody who-- who we find in the rooms in half an hour will be shot on the spot.
So don't try to do tricks or hide under the toilets, and the mattresses or anything like that.
Anybody rouse.
I had a few friends who said, they can't, they can't.
Because we had to-- we knew what it would be.
They should be all driven away like cattle, you know.
So he said that he can't walk.
He's going to hide.
He went up to the attic, and he tucked himself into some old ropes, or whatever, I don't know.
He stayed behind.
He said, I can't walk.
He was the one with malaria and didn't quite recover from it yet.
I still had my leg heavily bandaged.
There was still a bit of blood coming through.
But I put it real tight, the bandage.
I put on all my-- I purchased one more jumper.
So I had one jumper I had cut up, which was just around my kidneys, to keep the terrible cold out.
And the second jumper I put on.
And I purchased from my cigarette tickets, the boots.
So I had a fairly good shoe, which was a big advantage.
And then they drove us out.
And [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Since the Russians were getting very close.
Everybody got about half a bread, which was maybe-- maybe 750 grams of bread.
And everybody, as far as I can tell, got one tin, just like a Campbell's soup, a soup with some meat in it, some beef pieces.
That was the provision of food.
So we could put it in our pocket, the bread here, and half a bread here, half a bread there, and the meat in the middle.
March, big march.
And [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Five in a row, and we marched out.
That was early in the morning, 5 o'clock.
And we marched and marched, marched all day long.

And by the time we got into late afternoon, we-- we started to find the first bodies on the roadside, people who couldn't march fast enough in the previous group, people who sat down near a tree, to just lean against a tree [? to find ?] a little rest, they all shot through the head.
Later on I could-- we saw all the bodies.
But later on, we could actually see all the-- a Jewish man sitting on the roadside, and an SS man stops his BMW motorbike, and walks up to him.
And the Jewish man is doing, please, please.
I get up in a few minutes.
I just have a little rest.
No.
Shot him, shot him through the chest.
And they shot, every-- every few seconds somebody got shot, here or there.
Somebody who sat down in the middle of the road, of course, got shot right away.
You couldn't stop for one second.
If you're stopped for more than two seconds, you got shot.
And in front of us went a big group of girls, many young women.
And there you could see, first you could see the bloody tampons lying everywhere.
Then-- then you can see the girls lying around where the blood was trickling down their legs.
They're with periods, obviously.

And later on you could find the girls who had-- who had no periods, who just couldn't walk fast enough, who couldn't go as fast as the BMW motorbike, is a certain speed, even at the lowest level, it's going, you know.
And so the women were-- usually there was a brain in the snow.
And after the brain was the woman.
Brain, woman.
It was where the brain flew out, two meters to the left, and then the woman was lying next to it.
And then as we went on, all night we marched.
I remember seeing thousands upon thousands of mostly women, but there were men too, of course.
It was the first night we marched right through.
And the next day we marched right through.
And of course people used up all their food by then, with no more food.
But we could eat a bit of snow now and then.
And then we arrived the night of the second night.
We arrived to a kind of a farm.
And there was a building where it was usually-- would housed the cows, a building which had a lot of straw in it.
And I was lucky enough to get into the building.
And I climbed up on a-- on a-- a few-- a step ladder.
And there was a-- it was a building for pigs and cows.
I don't know what the English word for it is for such a building.
Anyhow, I was lying in the straw, and just stretching out.
And another big group of Polish Jewish people came.
And they took over the building.
And they talked Yiddish to each other.
We in Budapest did not speak Yiddish.
I couldn't speak Yiddish.
They talked to me.
And something-- they asked me from which village am I coming.
And I-- I couldn't understand it.
I couldn't reply in Yiddish.
So they said, ah, you're not Yiddish.
You're not one of us.
Out with you.
They got hold of me, and they threw me out of the building.
Maybe they assumed that I'm a Christian.
Because in their-- in their world, anybody who doesn't speak Yiddish is not a Jew.
But of course, in my world, in Budapest, nobody spoke Yiddish.
And they were-- we were still Jews.
So I guess they assumed that I must be a-- one of the Christian prisoners, maybe be a Christian Communist or somebody like that.
So they threw me out.
But it was all for the best.
Because they threw me out-- outside of the building.
I was outside in the bitter cold, very cold.
And I were standing around.
I took off my shoes.
I-- Also people lie down, they froze to-- they died.
They froze to death.
I didn't lie down.
So I make sure.
I watch out.
I can't lie down.
Because then I froze to death.
So I'm just rubbing my toes, and putting back my sock, and putting back my shoe, and was leaning against the building.

And the inside the next building, there was a group of Wehrmacht soldiers, ordinary soldiers, 50-, 55-, 60-year-old soldiers who were called out, not fit enough to fight the Russians, but good enough to-- to guard the Jewish slaves.
So there was this German man who was probably a member of the Social Democratic Party or maybe even a communist.
And he said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
Which means, are you hungry.
I said, oh yes, I'm very hungry.
Oh, well come in.
Come here.
So I stepped into his building, I mean wooden hut.
It was a wooden hut, really, so the snow didn't fall.
It was covered from the snow, and had some windows.
And-- and then he gave me half of his dinner.
It was just some ham and eggs and sausages, and big chunk of bread.
So, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Are you still hungry?
Can I have some more, you know?
It very kind of him.
And so the people inside, they had no-- they had no food.
But by kicking me out, I-- I-- I got-- I got a good meal there.
[DRINKS] The only other event of that night was that one of the boys who couldn't get into the building was outside.
He had to have a-- he had to relieve himself.
So he went around the other building, and didn't want to do it in front of everybody.
So he went around the building.
And one of the SS men was just standing around there.
And he assumed that this man is trying to sneak away and escape.
But he didn't want to escape.
He just wanted to urinate. so he shot him through the stomach.
He shot him through the stomach.
He fell into the snow.
That was all six meters from me.
I could have-- I could have been that man.
I could have been him.
So he was there.
He was-- he was still conscious.
He was crying for help.
Help, help.
You know, later on, he's just crying for water.
And some other people that wanted to help him.
It was [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Leave him there.
He has to be an example to everybody who-- any bloody Jews who tried to escape, they could see that's the way they are going to die.
And he was moaning all night, crying for water, and help, and water, and water.
They let him bleed to death there.
All night, he was lying there, even in the morning.
And so I remember that very well.
Because I was-- I was-- me and him, there were two of us in that part of-- of the yard.
And the next morning sun gets 6 o'clock.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
People who couldn't get into the buildings, they froze to death.
They just got one more bullet to make sure that they're not pretending to be frozen.
They were really frozen.
But just to make sure, they just had the bullets in the neck or in the head.
So then we walked again all day long.
And it became harder and harder to walk.
Because, you know, especially people who didn't eat.
I had, you know, something to eat.
The next evening we were herded into a big soccer stadium.
The SS was just guarding the outside.
Inside you could do what you like to do.
And most people were so tired they just lied down in the snow.
It was a very cold.
And my friend was next to me.
And he lied down in the snow.
He said, oh, I need this rest.
I said, just be careful.
Because about minus 20 you could freeze to death.
Oh, he said, that will be good.
I'm looking forward to death, he says.
I-- I can't go through one more day of walking.
I-- I'm terribly weak.
I got no strength left.
So I said, I tell you, if you don't-- if you do that, you will be dead.
OK, so what?
And he was dead.
He lied down.
Most-- most of the people lied down.
I wouldn't say most, but big numbers lied down in the snow.
And they-- they froze to death for sure.
Well, I was up again all night.
This was my third night up without any sleep.
During the walk, I think I must have slept while I was walking, just a few seconds now and then.
And so the snow, in the morning SS, new SS men came after breakfast.
They had their good breakfast.
And they slept in their bed.
They were [INAUDIBLE] live.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
And all these frozen people [LAUGHS] spending-- that's-- spending 12 hours, in minus 20 in the snow.
Imagine how bright people felt.
And now, I had to go, had to go.
And other people were left.
They just went around for 10, 15 minutes shooting all the people who were already probably dead anyhow, but just to make sure that not one should fall into the Russian hands.
So we had to march all day again.
And now comes miracle number maybe six or seven.
When we were marching all day on the highway, which was leading between big forest.
We were approaching the Polish-Czechoslovak border, and near a town called Rybnik.
Anyhow, we were approaching the border.
In the-- in those mountains, again, we were away from the plains.
We were going up hill in the mountains.
And I was getting very, very tired, very, very exhausted, maybe from lack of sleep or maybe from tiredness or whatever.
And I was in the middle of the column.
I could hear behind me, you know, maybe 300 meters away the shooting, you know, people stopped, always shot.
And I could feel a pain in my chest.
My heart was in real pain.
What do call a stitch-- or you say it.
It was getting worse and worse.
So I slowed down.
And I still walked, make sure I walked.
But just gently falling back.
Not-- I didn't walk with my previous partners.
I just fall back slowly.
And I got more back, and more back.
Then I got-- I look back.
And I see I'm nearly at the end of the column.
And about 20, 30 meters behind me, just like from here to the swimming pool, I could see people sitting, stopping, bang.
Pew, pew.
The SS men had a real target practice there.
They shot every minute, six, 10, 15 people.
And I thought to myself, oh well.
Not-- not a big deal, another two minutes I will have to stop.
I can't go on much longer, whether two minutes or six minutes or 10 minutes.
So what?
I get a bullet.
It won't be such a big deal.
So that's where I realize my friends in Budapest, after they will never know where I died.
Because there's no-- I had no friends around me by that stage.
So all right.
I'm going to die in 10 minutes.
So, all right.
OK.
So it's not bad-- not-- not very painful.
And so I go to the very end.
I can see the picture.
I could hear the BMW motorbike behind me, just a couple of meters away, maybe 10 meters behind me.
The BMW sometimes stopped, he got out, shot another two, three people.
And then when I was just at the end, so I was just ready to sit down and get the bullet.
And then two fairly strong Polish Jews were, each one was holding me under the shoulder, trying to help me a bit.
And they said, come on.
You can go a bit better.
You can-- you can go a bit more.
You're still fairly young.
So I was, no.
Don't worry.
No need to pull me.
I mean, I can't go much further anyway.
I will have to drop in a few minutes.
Oh, come on.
After the war you can go to Israel, and you can still live.
The war is nearly finished.
It would be a shame to die now.
And but-- I said, it's useless.
I-- even if you pull me another 100, 200 meters.
I-- I just can't walk.
I've got a terrific pain in my chest.
They said, come on, try.
Try harder.
And the next-- that went on for about two minutes.
And the next thing what happened is there was machine gun fire from the wood.
The Communist partisans launched an attack against the column.
All they could see is a lot of people marching.
They didn't know whether they're Wehrmacht soldiers or what.
They were in the forest.
But they opened up from both sides with machine guns.
And the SS, [SPEAKING GERMAN]!
Whoever lifts-- lie in the snow.
Whoever lifts up his head or his arm, because they worried that we start to run out to the partisans.
That was their big worry.
So anybody who lifts up his head gets a bullet right away.
No questions asked.
Lie in the snow.
[LAUGHS] The beauty of it was they didn't have to tell me to twice to-- to lie in the snow.
I just lied down in the snow, fell asleep.

And the next second-- for a while I could hear-- I mean not the second, but for the next minute I could hear for a minute the machine guns firing, the SS men taking up position with their machine guns.
A group, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. 20 men attack this forest. 30 men attack that forest.
You know, the orders went out from the officers.
And [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You know, and then I fell asleep.
And I had a long sleep.
They-- they had a regular battle.
They went into the forest.
The forest people were shooting at us, bullets flying everywhere.
And then after I don't know how much time, I had a good sound sleep.
But eventually the two Israelis told to me, get up.
Get up.
They just said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And I got up.
And to my amazement, the pain was completely gone.
I had one hour of sleep.
It makes a big difference, you know.
And I could walk.
And after [INAUDIBLE], we arrived again to a circus stadium.
Same story again.
I could get near a building this time where it was in the stands or something where it was a bit of a cover from the-- so 10 people close together.
It was kind of warmed up.
The next day, the next morning-- so I survived this.
This was the nearest I came, apart from the selection on the 1st of October, which was a very close shave, of course.
But this was a sure thing.
One more minute, if the partisans opened fire not that day, or not that-- that day but half an hour later, I get my bullet, for sure.
That's an absolute miracle.
I still dream about it nearly every-- every week.
I have this dream.
Anyhow, next day we arrived to a town very near to the Czech border, near Moravska Ostrava.
And there was a beautiful long train with open coal wagons, open.
No-- no tops just sides, but nothing on the top.
[GERMAN]!
Every coal wagon, 100 people in.
They pushed us in like-- like sardines. 100 people, 100 people, 100 in here, 100 in there.
There was no question about food, of course.
[LAUGHS] Snow, you could eat the snow.
And then the train started to go.
It went through Czechoslovakia.
It went through, from Moravska Ostrava, right through across all of Czechoslovakia into Austria.
And that journey, well, I was only half conscious.
Because I was better off than some of my friends.
Because they-- they just went-- they went crazy.
Some of them talked incoherently.
Some of them were just crying, mother, mother.
Some of them just died.
Other people, who sat down for one minute, were-- were trampled to death right away.
Because if one sits down, and 100 people are pressing, not that you want to.
But the others are pressing you.
You had to step on him.
Later on, it got easier.
Because by that time, 15, 20 people were lying dead.
Later on, half of the cabin was dead.
So then we had more-- we could sit on-- put together three dead bodies, and we could sit on them.
But then first, you had to survive the first half a day, first 10 hours.
This was crucial.
Because if you fell down then, then crowd stands on you.
So if you survived that, then you had it made.
Because then you could make a seat from three or four bodies.
You could rest.
And then the snow was falling all the time, of course.
People froze to death.
I-- I still kept working on my arms to make sure that I don't freeze to death.
But the conditions were very bad.
I had temperature already then from the cold, you know, exposure.
And my cheeks were swollen, and I was-- I wasn't quite conscious, but still alive anyhow.
And eventually we got to the suburb of Vienna, outer suburb of Vienna.
So it was a, you know, very busy place.
And by some miracle, the train always had to stop.
Because military trains always had first preference.
It's just bloody, dying Jews in-- in the open covered in snow that they can wait in the snow.
So we could-- the train always stopped.
And the other trains, three trains went through.
And every train was more important than our train, obviously.
So we just stopped under a-- a railway over-- pedestrians going over, in the suburb.
You could see both sides of the street.
There was a movie on the one side.
And a big crowd came out from the movies.
And-- and they came out, young men with young girls, you know, mostly young men.
Because the older men were all obviously in the army.
But a young couple came up on the overpass and they came up and-- a girl say to boy, look.
Shudders. ?] Look at that that.
Look what condition these people are in.
Oh, they're all criminals anyhow, or whatever.
You know.
You know, they're covered in snow, with their bodies-- I can-- I can imagine what-- they could-- he was just two meters over.
I was in the open high wagon without covers, so he could see.
You could look in.
And he was going over there.
And the girl was eating a chocolate bar, or some candy bar anyhow, you know, not chocolate, but imitation chocolate.
And the boys was sipping strawberry juice, strawberry or lemonade or something like this, strawberry.
And he was sipping it.
And I and a couple others started to beg for him to give us something to drink, water or give us a drink.
And he-- he let the almost full strawberry bottle go, and I caught it.
I caught the bottle.
You know, he was just-- he bent down and let it drop.
I caught the bottle.
And like mad I started to drink the sugar, you know, sugar in it.
So I was drinking like mad.
And by that time, two or three other men who were still in reasonably good condition see what I'm doing.
And they quickly jumped on me and took the-- took the bottle from me.
But I had about 20 seconds head start.
By that time I drank as much as I could.
Then they finished the bottle.
Anyway, that-- that's all I remember.
And then the train went on.
And one of my friends went completely crazy.
His ulcer perforated.
His stomach was open, with blood running everywhere.
And there was more and more dead people.
Anyhow, eventually the train arrived to the station, which was cold.
We read it out, Mauthausen.
It meant nothing for us, just a German name, Mauthausen.
And the fresh group of 50 SS men, just must have finished their breakfast.
Because they were in fantastic moods.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
[LAUGHS] Quick, quick, quick out.
Stand to fives.
Fives, you're lucky if you could get out from the bloody wagon.
Jump down from a high platform to the ground, great difficulty.
Anybody that doesn't come out will be shot on the spot.
So that was additional incentive for me to-- to climb out.
With some difficulty I got out, and tried to stretch my legs.
And quick.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
Stand to attention, rows of five.
And then they picked out about 20 young men, not me, luckily, the others who had to go up to the wagons.
And [GERMAN].
Because they had to-- they had to throw out all the firewood, the dead bodies, which were cluttering up the lovely wagons.
The wagons had to be used for other purposes.
So they quickly we're throwing out all the bodies, live or dead, doesn't matter.
They're throwing them out on the concrete.
Half of them are still alive, but unconscious or frozen a bit, or hungry or whatever.
Just throw them out on the concrete.
And just to make sure, the SS men went around and pumped two or three bullets into each lying body.
The lucky ones were dead already anyhow.
So-- anyhow, quick march.
And so we started our quick march up the hill, a very steep hill.
I've been back four times since then.
From the railway station to the Mauthausen camp on the mountain, up on the mountain.
It takes me about one hour to do it now, in a-- in a good condition.
But then it took about two hours probably, for the people who survived, and who stopped on the way, got their bullet, of course.
But we marched up all the way to the camp, in the hope that being a new camp, so we should be disinfected.
We'll get a shower.
And probably we'll get a hot cabbage soup.
Which was, everybody was starving after a hot soup.
It doesn't matter if there's nothing in it, just hot, something hot into our stomach.
So we try to get up as fast as we can.
But the trouble is another transport arrived just before us.
And there were already about 10,000 people jammed in.
And when we came in, they jammed us in too.
And before we could get any food, according to the German rules, you have to be clean.
You have to be [GERMAN].
You had to be uniform down, shower with soap, and new uniform.
And there's only one shower section with six shower heads.
So they could only let in about 10 or 12 people at a time.
And there were thousands of people in front of me.
So we got no-- no hot soup on that they, and all night standing in the snow.
It was terrible.
On the mountaintop in Austria, in the middle of winter in January, it's very cold and windy, as well.
So people sat down, froze to death.
OK.
Be it.
My friends, my delirious friends, who said, mother, mother.
I'm coming to you.
Come to me.
And I said, look, if you lie down like that.
He was already delirious.
And he says, Frank, here.
Take this piece of bread.
I was saving it all the last four days.
But you can have it now.
I-- I don't need it anymore.
I hope I freeze to death.
And he did.
He did.
And the next day, the next day some people managed to get one barrel of hot coffee-- I mean hot water, which I got one cup of it.
And the next day again, the next night went past standing outside.
We still couldn't get to the shower room.
And then came the third morning.
And I had a brilliant idea.
I was very sick.
I had temperature.
But I had a brilliant idea.
I met the other doctor and the dentist from Auschwitz.
And we were-- we realized we are very far back from the shower place.
But we realized if we don't get through before nightfall, before the night comes-- because nighttime it's much colder than the daytime-- then we shall freeze to death the next night.
Because we were very, very weak.
So we thought, we have to make an effort to get to the shower.
And the thing to do is to put on our Auschwitz band-- arm bands, and try to look as official as possible.
And-- and we started to shout in the best [GERMAN] like the Berlin accent.
[GERMAN], you know, make room.
And, you know, doctor coming.
[GERMAN].
Make room for the interpreter.
And people were already half unconscious anyhow, the ones who were not dead yet.
And so we could step over dead bodies and half-dead people.
And [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You know, and we just gently pushed them aside.
And we jumped the queue.
We jumped the queue so to say, because we were very, very much back.
We knew that we are dying if we don't get forward.
Well--
Oh.
[LAUGHS]
